zotero.abstractNote: The brain's intrinsic neural timescales (INTs) are thought to reflect the temporal integration window of neural processing. While previous studies have shown that INTs are organized along a sensory-to-association cortical hierarchy, it remains unclear whether they are directly related to sensory processing. Here, we investigated this relationship by examining INTs in patients with disorders of consciousness (DOC), a condition characterized by impaired sensory processing and awareness. We found that INTs were significantly altered in DOC patients compared to healthy controls, particularly in sensory-related cortical areas. Furthermore, the degree of INT alteration was correlated with the level of behavioral impairment, as measured by the Coma Recovery Scale-Revised. These findings suggest that INTs are not only a fundamental property of cortical organization but are also closely linked to the integrity of sensory processing and, by extension, to the level of consciousness.
